original_hypothesis.title: Bonding curve getPrice underflows for supply=0 and amount>1, preventing multi-mint bootstrap and causing unintended DoS for initial buy-with-name/presale setup
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: getPrice uses (supply - 1 + amount) where supply is uint256. When supply=0 and amount>1, (supply - 1) underflows and reverts in Solidity 0.8.7, breaking initial purchases that attempt to buy more than 1 token (e.g., buyCurvesTokenWithName with amount>1).
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a deterministic correctness defect that blocks expected protocol flows (bootstrapping supply). While not direct theft, it can break launches and force multiple transactions.
affected_functions: buyCurvesTokenWithName(address curvesTokenSubject, uint256 amount, string memory name, string memory symbol)
buyCurvesTokenForPresale(address curvesTokenSubject, uint256 amount, uint256 startTime, bytes32 merkleRoot, uint256 maxBuy)

inventory_refs.function: buyCurvesTokenWithName(address curvesTokenSubject, uint256 amount, string memory name, string memory symbol)

proof_of_signal.pattern: getPrice computes `supply - 1 + amount` and `2 * (supply - 1 + amount) + 1`; underflows when supply==0 and amount>1

preconditions: Caller attempts initial buy for a new subject with supply=0 and amount>1

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: unchecked precondition on uint arithmetic with supply-1
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: no branch handling supply==0 for amount>1

analysis_questions: Is protocol design intended to restrict first purchase to amount==1, or is this accidental?
Which user-facing entrypoints allow supply==0 and arbitrary amount (buyCurvesTokenWithName, buyCurvesTokenForPresale)?
Are there UI/workflow mitigations, and do they match docs?
validation_requirements: Confirm whether subjects can still reach desired initial supply via sequential buys

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Griefing/DoS: subjects attempting to initialize with amount>1 will revert repeatedly until they discover the amount==1 limitation.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Limited; primarily operational disruption and potential extra gas cost

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No tests for getPrice at supply=0 and amount>1

validation_result.mechanism: arithmetic underflow in getPrice for supply=0 and amount>1
validation_result.impact_summary: getPrice reverts when supply=0 and amount>1 due to uint256 underflow at (supply - 1), preventing subjects from buying more than 1 token in the initial purchase via buyCurvesTokenWithName or buyCurvesTokenForPresale.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The getPrice function at Curves.sol lines 60-68 computes sum2 using (supply - 1 + amount). The ternary guard only covers the exact case supply==0 && amount==1. When supply==0 and amount>1, the else branch executes. Due to left-to-right evaluation and same-precedence of addition/subtraction, (supply - 1 + amount) evaluates as ((supply - 1) + amount). With supply=0 in Solidity 0.8.7's checked arithmetic, (0 - 1) causes an arithmetic underflow and reverts before the addition of amount can occur. This is a deterministic code defect that prevents multi-token bootstrapping of new curves.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof_of_signal is correct. Lines 60-68 of Curves.sol contain the getPrice function with the vulnerable pattern. The ternary condition on line 63 (`supply == 0 && amount == 1`) only handles the single-token case; when amount > 1, the else branch is reached and `supply - 1` underflows.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: When supply=0 and amount>1, the ternary condition (supply == 0 && amount == 1) is false, so the else branch executes. The expression (supply - 1 + amount) evaluates left-to-right as ((0 - 1) + amount), and (0 - 1) underflows in Solidity 0.8.7 checked arithmetic, causing an unconditional revert.

calling_sequence: 1. buyCurvesTokenWithName(curvesTokenSubject, amount=2, name, symbol) called by subject
2. supply = curvesTokenSupply[curvesTokenSubject] = 0, check supply != 0 passes (it IS 0, so continues)
4. supply = 0, check (supply > 0 || curvesTokenSubject == msg.sender) passes since subject is msg.sender
7. sum2: supply == 0 && amount == 1 → false (amount is 2), enters else branch
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The path from buyCurvesTokenWithName → _buyCurvesToken → getPrice is deterministic. When a subject tries to initialize their curve by buying more than 1 token, getPrice(0, N) where N>1 always reverts. The same path exists via buyCurvesTokenForPresale → _buyCurvesToken → getPrice. The ternary guard on line 63 only protects the exact case (supply==0 && amount==1), leaving all other supply==0 cases with amount>1 vulnerable to underflow.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: The hardhat.config.ts confirms Solidity compiler version 0.8.7, which has checked arithmetic by default. This confirms the underflow will revert rather than wrap around.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: This is not an attack per se but a deterministic code defect. Any token subject attempting to bootstrap their curve with amount > 1 in a single transaction will fail. The scenario: Subject calls buyCurvesTokenWithName(subject, 5, 'MyToken', 'MTK') to create their curve with 5 initial tokens. The transaction reverts with arithmetic underflow. The subject must instead buy exactly 1 token first, then buy additional tokens in a separate transaction. This exposes the subject to frontrunning on the second transaction, as other users can buy between the two transactions, increasing the price the subject pays for their remaining tokens.
validation_result.impact_assessment: MEDIUM severity. The defect prevents multi-token initialization of curves in a single atomic transaction. Subjects must buy exactly 1 token initially, then buy more in subsequent transactions. This creates a non-atomic bootstrap process that is vulnerable to frontrunning/sandwich attacks between transactions. The workaround exists (sequential buys) but introduces MEV risk and extra gas costs. The defect also affects buyCurvesTokenForPresale, meaning presale bootstrapping with amount>1 fails.
recommended_mitigations: Change the ternary condition on sum2 from `supply == 0 && amount == 1` to `supply == 0` and adjust the formula to handle the full range: when supply==0, sum2 should compute the sum from index 0 to (amount-1), which equals ((amount - 1) * amount * (2 * (amount - 1) + 1)) / 6
Alternatively, rewrite the formula to avoid (supply - 1) when supply could be 0, e.g., by using supply + amount - 1 rewritten as (supply + amount) - 1 when supply + amount >= 1
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy Curves with factory and fee splitter. 2. Call getPrice(0, 2) directly — expect revert. 3. Call buyCurvesTokenWithName(msg.sender, 2, 'Test', 'TST') from subject address with sufficient ETH — expect revert. 4. Call buyCurvesTokenWithName(msg.sender, 1, 'Test', 'TST') — expect success. 5. Call buyCurvesToken(msg.sender, 1) — expect success (supply now 1, no underflow).
uncertainties: Whether the protocol designers intended this as a deliberate constraint (buy exactly 1 first) or it is an accidental limitation. The code does not document this restriction.
additional_testing_needed: Test getPrice(0, N) for N=2,3,...,100 to confirm all revert
Test that getPrice(1, N) works correctly for N>=1 to confirm the issue is isolated to supply=0
Test the full buyCurvesTokenForPresale flow with amount>1 to confirm it also reverts
